message:
feat(demo_showcase): Wire content pack into App runtime state

Add DemoContent struct and wire it into App initialization:
- DemoFile: name, language hint, text content
- DemoLinks: repo, source, docs, unicode_ref URLs
- MetricParams: target_fps for deterministic metrics
- DemoContent: bundles files, links, seed_logs, metric_params

App content wiring:
- current_file_idx: tracks active editor file
- logs: Vec<LogEntry> for log panel (starts with seed_logs)
- target_fps: for metrics computation
- metrics: updated each frame via tick()

New App methods:
- with_content(): initialize with custom DemoContent
- current_file(), current_file_name(), current_file_language()
- next_file(), prev_file(): file navigation
- add_log(): append to log stream
- update_metrics(): called in tick()

Added 15 tests for content wiring functionality.

Closes bd-hc0

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1ei","title":"demo_showcase spec: content pack (sample code, logs, links, unicode strings, metrics)","description":"# demo_showcase Spec — Content Pack

## Purpose

The demo needs a high-quality “content pack” so it looks like a real application and also proves correctness:

- realistic code to edit (highlighting + editing)
- logs that look like real systems output
- deterministic metrics for charts
- Unicode strings that prove grapheme + width correctness
- hyperlink URLs to prove OSC 8 integration

This bead defines the canonical content sets so every other bead can reference them without re-inventing strings.

## Content Sets

### 1) Editor Sample Content (highlighting + editing)

Primary buffer: a small Rust module that contains:

- `struct`/`enum` + `impl`
- lifetimes + generics
- doc comments + line comments
- `match`, `if let`, `Result`/`?`
- strings with escapes + raw strings
- a few `TODO:` style comments (to look realistic)

Secondary buffer (optional): Markdown with fenced Rust code blocks to prove tokenizer switching (if supported by the built-in registries).

**Important constraint:** keep the editor sample mostly to single-codepoint characters. Avoid multi-codepoint graphemes (ZWJ emoji, combining sequences) in the editor text itself, because `EditorView::render_to` does not use the grapheme pool path.

### 2) Logs Content (event stream)

The logs panel should show an event stream with:

- timestamps
- `INFO/WARN/ERROR` levels
- subsystem tags (`renderer`, `input`, `preview`, `tour`, etc.)
- a few “actionable” entries that are linkified via OSC 8

Example (conceptual):

- `[22:05:12] INFO  renderer: diff=124 cells, bytes=3.1KB`
- `[22:05:13] WARN  input: focus lost (paused)`
- `[22:05:14] ERROR preview: simulated failure (press R to retry)`

### 3) Metrics Content (charts + animation)

Metrics must be deterministic (derived from time + fixed parameters):

- FPS + frame time
- “CPU usage” synthetic waveform
- “memory bytes” synthetic counter
- a periodic “pulse” value for glow animations

No randomness (so tour mode is reproducible and tests can assert on values).

### 4) Unicode / Width Prove‑Out Strings (pool-rendered)

These strings must be rendered using the grapheme pool path (`buffer_with_pool` + `draw_text_with_pool` / `render_to_with_pool`) so correctness is visible.

Include at least:

- CJK wide chars: `漢字かなカナ`
- single-codepoint emoji: `🎉👍😀`
- multi-codepoint ZWJ emoji: `👨‍👩‍👧` / `👩‍💻` / `🧑‍🚀`
- combining marks: `a\u{0301}` (á) and `n\u{0303}` (ñ)

Also include a fixed “width ruler” line under each string so a viewer can see alignment and width decisions.

### 5) Hyperlink URLs (OSC 8)

Use stable URLs that are obviously legitimate:

- `https://github.com/anomalyco/opentui`
- `https://github.com/anomalyco/opentui/tree/main/src`
- `https://unicode.org/reports/tr11/`

The exact URLs are less important than:

- they’re real
- they’re visually recognizable
- at least one is rendered in a log row and in a “docs”/help context

## Why This Matters

A demo’s credibility lives or dies on its content. If the strings are artificial or the logs look fake, the viewer won’t trust the engine.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:

 {"id":"bd-1fu","title":"demo_showcase UI: sidebar nav (scrollable list + scissor + mouse selection)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Sidebar Navigation

## Purpose

Implement a scrollable sidebar that proves:

- nested scissor clipping
- scroll offsets
- mouse wheel scrolling
- click hit testing per row

…and that drives the app’s **Section** navigation coherently.

## Content

Sidebar items (example):

- Overview
- Editor
- Preview
- Logs
- Unicode
- Performance
- Settings

## Behavior

- Up/Down selects item (when focused)
- Mouse wheel scrolls list (when hovered)
- Click selects item and focuses sidebar

Navigation semantics:

- Selecting an item updates `App.section` (see state machine bead).
- Navigating to a section also sets a sensible default focus:
  - Editor -> focus editor
  - Preview/Unicode/Performance -> focus preview
  - Logs -> focus logs

In compact layout, `section` determines which panel is shown full-screen.

## Rendering

- Panel border (focused highlights)
- Header “NAV”
- Scrollbar indicator
- Selected row uses selection background (alpha overlay)

## HitGrid

- Register a hit area per visible row.
- ID encoding scheme: `HIT_SIDEBAR_BASE + row_index`.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:

## Why Tour Mode Exists

The showcase must be *demoable without a human driver*.

Tour mode provides:

- a deterministic sequence for recording videos
- a “guided explanation” overlay for first-time users
- an automatic prove-out of advanced features

## Tour Model

- Tour is a sequence of steps: `Step { title, description, duration_ms, action }`
- Each step can:
  - mutate app state (select panel, scroll, open overlay, type text)
  - trigger an animation or highlight effect
  - pop a toast describing what’s happening
- Determinism:
  - fixed durations
  - seeded animation parameters (no randomness; if “random”, derive from step index)

## Proposed Steps (Feature-Proving Sequence)

1. **Welcome + No Flicker**
   - Show title + smooth animated accent bar.
   - Toast: “Diff rendering + synchronized output (if supported).”

2. **Scissor-Clipped Sidebar Scroll**
   - Auto-scroll sidebar list inside clip rect.
   - Visual: scroll indicator.

3. **Focus + Hit Testing**
   - Simulate “clicking” top bar buttons (help/palette) and switching panel focus.
   - Visual: border glow moves.

4. **Command Palette (Glass Overlay)**
   - Open palette overlay.
   - Demonstrate opacity stack + blended background.

5. **Editor: Typing + Undo/Redo**
   - Insert a few lines.
   - Undo/redo quickly.
   - Toast: “Rope + undo groups.”

6. **Editor: Highlighting + Theme Switch**
   - Turn on syntax highlighting.
   - Switch theme (Dark → Light → High contrast).

7. **Bracketed Paste Proof**
   - Inject a synthetic Paste event into editor.
   - Toast: “Bracketed paste parsed and inserted.”

8. **Unicode + Width + Grapheme Pool**
   - Render a line containing:
     - CJK wide chars
     - single-codepoint emoji
     - multi-codepoint ZWJ emoji (must require pool)
   - Show a small “width ruler” under it.

9. **Preview: PixelBuffer + Supersampling**
   - Animate a small pixel-art + sparkline chart.

10. **Alpha Blending / Opacity**
   - Draw a translucent “modal” over preview with a drop-shadow-ish effect.

11. **Hyperlinks (OSC 8)**
   - Render clickable links (docs/GitHub) in the logs panel.
   - Visual: link styling + hover/pressed feedback.

12. **Finale: Performance Overlay**
   - Show render stats (fps, changed cells, bytes estimate).
   - End with a “you can build this” message.

## User Controls

- Start/stop: `Ctrl+T`
- Next: `Enter` / `Space`
- Prev: `Backspace`
- Exit tour: `Esc`

Unattended runs:

- When started with `--exit-after-tour`, the final tour step completion triggers a clean process exit (useful for recordings and PTY E2E).

## Acceptance Style

 {"id":"bd-1hd","title":"Integration Example","description":"## Overview
Create a working example demonstrating the full rendering loop with input handling.

## Scope
Simple text editor example showing:
- Terminal setup/cleanup
- Input event loop
- Rendering pipeline
- Proper error handling
- Debug logging capability

## Implementation
```rust
// examples/simple_editor.rs
use opentui::prelude::*;
use std::io::{stdin, stdout, Read, Write};
use std::env;

fn main() -> Result<(), Box<dyn std::error::Error>> {
    // Check for debug mode
    let debug = env::var("OPENTUI_DEBUG").is_ok();
    
    // Setup logging
    if debug {
        eprintln!("[DEBUG] OpenTUI Simple Editor starting");
    }
    
    // Get terminal size
    let (width, height) = terminal_size()?;
    if debug {
        eprintln!("[DEBUG] Terminal size: {}x{}", width, height);
    }
    
    // Setup
    let _guard = enable_raw_mode()?;
    let mut renderer = Renderer::new(width as u32, height as u32)?;
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("Hello, OpenTUI!\n\nPress Ctrl+Q to quit.");
    let mut view = EditorView::new(buffer);
    view.set_line_numbers(true);
    view.set_wrap_mode(WrapMode::Word);
    
    if debug {
        eprintln!("[DEBUG] Editor initialized");
    }
    
    // Event loop
    let mut parser = InputParser::new();
    let mut input_buf = [0u8; 64];
    let mut frame_count = 0u64;

    loop {
        frame_count += 1;
        
        // Render
        renderer.clear();
        view.render_to(renderer.buffer_mut(), 0, 0, width as u32, height as u32);
        renderer.present(&mut stdout())?;
        
        if debug && frame_count % 60 == 0 {
            let cursor = view.edit_buffer().cursor();
            eprintln!("[DEBUG] Frame {} - cursor: row={} col={} offset={}",
                frame_count, cursor.row, cursor.col, cursor.offset);
        }

        // Handle input
        let n = stdin().read(&mut input_buf)?;
        
        if debug {
            eprintln!("[DEBUG] Input: {} bytes, hex: {}",
                n, input_buf[..n].iter().map(|b| format!("{:02x}", b)).collect::<Vec<_>>().join(" "));
        }

        for event in parser.parse(&input_buf[..n]) {
            if debug {
                eprintln!("[DEBUG] Event: {:?}", event);
            }
            
            match event {
                InputEvent::Key(KeyEvent { code: KeyCode::Char('q'), modifiers })
                    if modifiers.contains(Modifiers::CTRL) => {
                    if debug {
                        eprintln!("[DEBUG] Ctrl+Q detected, exiting");
                    }
                    return Ok(());
                }
                InputEvent::Key(key) => {
                    view.handle_key(key);
                    view.scroll_to_cursor(width as u32, height as u32);
                }
                InputEvent::Mouse(mouse) => {
                    view.handle_mouse(mouse);
                }
                InputEvent::Resize { width: w, height: h } => {
                    if debug {
                        eprintln!("[DEBUG] Resize to {}x{}", w, h);
                    }
                    renderer.resize(w as u32, h as u32);
                    view.set_viewport(0, 0, w as u32, h as u32);
                }
                _ => {}
            }
        }
    }
}
```

- examples/simple_editor.rs - basic editor example (~100 lines)
- examples/README.md - example documentation

### README.md Content
```markdown
# OpenTUI Examples

## Simple Editor

A minimal text editor demonstrating the OpenTUI rendering loop.

### Running

```bash
cargo run --example simple_editor
```

### Debug Mode

Enable verbose logging:

```bash
OPENTUI_DEBUG=1 cargo run --example simple_editor 2>debug.log
```

### Controls

- Arrow keys: Move cursor
- Ctrl+Q: Quit
- Ctrl+S: Save (if implemented)
- Page Up/Down: Scroll
- Home/End: Line start/end

### What This Demonstrates

1. **Terminal Setup**: Raw mode, alternate screen
2. **Input Handling**: Parsing ANSI sequences into events
3. **Rendering Loop**: Double-buffered, diff-based updates
4. **Resource Cleanup**: RAII guard restores terminal state
```

## Testing Requirements

### Manual Test Checklist (with logging verification)
Run with OPENTUI_DEBUG=1 and verify:
- [ ] Terminal size logged on startup
- [ ] Input bytes logged for each keypress
- [ ] Events logged (Key, Mouse, Resize)
- [ ] Cursor position logged periodically
- [ ] Ctrl+Q exit logged
- [ ] No panic on resize
- [ ] Terminal restored on exit (even after panic)

### Automated Smoke Test (tests/e2e/example_smoke.rs)
```rust
#[test]
fn test_example_compiles() {
    // Just verify the example compiles
    use std::process::Command;
    
    let output = Command::new("cargo")
        .args(["build", "--example", "simple_editor"])
        .output()
        .expect("Failed to run cargo");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Build stdout: {}", String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout));
    eprintln!("[TEST] Build stderr: {}", String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr));
    
    assert!(output.status.success(), "Example should compile");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: Example compiles successfully");
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Example compiles and runs
- [ ] Demonstrates full input/output loop
- [ ] Handles Ctrl+Q to quit
- [ ] Properly cleans up terminal on exit
- [ ] Debug mode available via OPENTUI_DEBUG env var
- [ ] Debug logs show input bytes, events, and cursor state
- [ ] README explains how to run and debug
- [ ] No panic on common terminal operations

 {"id":"bd-1i7","title":"demo_showcase spec: resilience + degradation rules (small terminal, no truecolor, no mouse)","description":"# demo_showcase Spec — Resilience + Degradation Rules

## Goal

The demo should be impressive in ideal terminals, but also **never embarrassing** in constrained environments.

## Constraints to Handle

- Very small terminal sizes (e.g., 50×15)
- No truecolor (16/256-color fallback)
- No mouse reporting (or mouse disabled)
- No synchronized output support
- TTY missing (demo should refuse gracefully or run headless mode)

## Layout Degradation

- Below minimum width/height, switch to a “compact layout”:
  - collapse preview or logs
  - show a single focused panel full-screen with a minimal top/bottom bar
- Always keep a visible help hint: “F1 Help / Ctrl+Q Quit”.

## Rendering Degradation

- If colors are limited, keep contrast high:
  - prefer fewer accent colors
  - use bold/dim to provide hierarchy
- Avoid relying on italic/strikethrough.

## Input Degradation

- If mouse is disabled:
  - ensure keyboard navigation can do everything (Tab cycles focus, Enter activates).

## Failure Modes

- If `terminal_size()` fails or stdout isn’t a TTY:
  - print a clear message and exit non-zero
  - or offer `--headless-smoke` that runs deterministic render steps to a buffer and exits 0","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:

 {"id":"bd-1j0","title":"[EPIC] Comprehensive Benchmark Suite","description":"# Epic: Comprehensive Benchmark Suite

## Background & Motivation

OpenTUI claims to be a "high-performance terminal UI rendering engine." This claim must be backed by rigorous benchmarks. Currently, the project has only a single `buffer.rs` benchmark file with basic operations. We need:

1. **Performance baselines** - Know current performance for regression detection
2. **Optimization guidance** - Identify bottlenecks and hot paths
3. **Comparative data** - How does OpenTUI compare to alternatives?
4. **Documentation** - Performance characteristics for users making decisions

## Current State

The existing `benches/buffer.rs` covers:
- `buffer_clear` - Clearing the entire buffer
- `buffer_draw_text` - Drawing text strings
- `buffer_blend` - Alpha blending operations

This is a good start but doesn't cover many critical operations.

## Goals

1. Benchmark all major subsystems (buffer, text, renderer, color, unicode)
2. Establish performance baselines for CI regression detection
3. Provide realistic workload benchmarks (not just micro-benchmarks)
4. Document expected performance characteristics

## Benchmark Categories

### 1. Buffer Operations (expand existing)
- Clear operations at various sizes
- Cell set/get operations
- fill_rect at various sizes
- draw_text with different string lengths
- draw_box with various box styles
- Scissor stack operations
- Opacity stack operations

### 2. Color Operations
- Rgba creation methods
- Porter-Duff blending
- Color conversions (HSV, hex, u8)
- Lerp operations

### 3. Text/Rope Operations
- TextBuffer creation and population
- Rope insertions (beginning, middle, end)
- Rope deletions
- Line iteration
- EditBuffer operations with undo/redo

### 4. Renderer Operations
- Buffer diff calculation
- ANSI sequence generation
- Full render cycle

### 5. Unicode Operations
- Grapheme iteration
- Display width calculation
- Mixed ASCII/emoji/CJK text

### 6. Input Parsing
- Key event parsing
- Mouse event parsing
- Escape sequence parsing

### 7. Realistic Workloads
- "Editor frame" - Scroll + render 1000 lines
- "Animation frame" - Update 100 moving elements
- "Dashboard refresh" - Update 5 panels with data

## Design Principles

- **Criterion framework** - Already in use, provides statistical analysis
- **Realistic sizes** - Test at 80x24, 120x40, 200x60 terminal sizes
- **Parameterized benchmarks** - Same test at multiple scales
- **Comparison groups** - Group related benchmarks for comparison

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All major subsystems have benchmarks
- [ ] CI runs benchmarks on main branch
- [ ] Performance regression >10% triggers alert
- [ ] Benchmark results documented
- [ ] Realistic workload benchmarks included

## Non-Goals

- Comparison with other TUI libraries (complex, politically sensitive)
- Optimization work (that's separate tasks based on benchmark findings)
- Platform-specific benchmarks

## Dependencies

None - benchmarks can be written against existing code.

## Estimated Scope

~1000-1500 LOC across benchmark files.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T01:13:11.909043295Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T17:54:45.216276374Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T17:54:45.216209799Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["benchmark","epic"]}

 {"id":"bd-gtp","title":"Add Line Duplication and Movement Methods","description":"## Overview
Add line duplication and movement methods to EditBuffer.

## Already Implemented (NO WORK NEEDED)
The following methods already exist in `src/text/edit.rs`:
- `delete_line()` at line 381 ✓
- `goto_line()` at line 292 ✓

## Scope (Methods to Add)
```rust
impl EditBuffer {
    /// Duplicate the current line (insert copy below).
    pub fn duplicate_line(&mut self) {
        let row = self.cursor.row;
        if let Some(line) = self.buffer.rope().line(row) {
            let line_text = line.to_string();
            let line_end = self.buffer.rope().line_to_char(row) + line.len_chars();
            
            // Insert newline + line content after current line
            let insert_pos = if line_text.ends_with('\n') {
                line_end
            } else {
                // At last line without trailing newline
                line_end
            };
            
            self.begin_edit_group();
            if !line_text.ends_with('\n') {
                self.insert_at(insert_pos, "\n");
            }
            self.insert_at(insert_pos + if line_text.ends_with('\n') { 0 } else { 1 }, &line_text);
            self.end_edit_group();
        }
    }

    /// Move current line up (swap with line above).
    pub fn move_line_up(&mut self) {
        if self.cursor.row == 0 {
            return; // Can't move first line up
        }
        
        let current_row = self.cursor.row;
        let prev_row = current_row - 1;
        
        self.begin_edit_group();
        // Get both lines
        let current_line = self.buffer.rope().line(current_row).map(|l| l.to_string());
        let prev_line = self.buffer.rope().line(prev_row).map(|l| l.to_string());
        
        if let (Some(curr), Some(prev)) = (current_line, prev_line) {
            // Delete both lines and reinsert in swapped order
            let start = self.buffer.rope().line_to_char(prev_row);
            let end = self.buffer.rope().line_to_char(current_row) + 
                self.buffer.rope().line(current_row).map(|l| l.len_chars()).unwrap_or(0);
            
            self.delete_range_offsets(start, end);
            self.insert_at(start, &format!("{}{}", curr, prev));
            
            // Move cursor to new position (one line up)
            self.goto_line(prev_row);
        }
        self.end_edit_group();
    }

    /// Move current line down (swap with line below).
    pub fn move_line_down(&mut self) {
        let current_row = self.cursor.row;
        let next_row = current_row + 1;
        
        if next_row >= self.buffer.len_lines() {
            return; // Can't move last line down
        }
        
        self.begin_edit_group();
        // Similar to move_line_up but swap in opposite direction
        let current_line = self.buffer.rope().line(current_row).map(|l| l.to_string());
        let next_line = self.buffer.rope().line(next_row).map(|l| l.to_string());
        
        if let (Some(curr), Some(next)) = (current_line, next_line) {
            let start = self.buffer.rope().line_to_char(current_row);
            let end = self.buffer.rope().line_to_char(next_row) + 
                self.buffer.rope().line(next_row).map(|l| l.len_chars()).unwrap_or(0);
            
            self.delete_range_offsets(start, end);
            self.insert_at(start, &format!("{}{}", next, curr));
            
            // Move cursor to new position (one line down)
            self.goto_line(next_row);
        }
        self.end_edit_group();
    }
}
```

```rust
#[test]
fn test_duplicate_line() {
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("line 1\nline 2\nline 3");
    buffer.goto_line(1);  // Move to "line 2"
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Initial text:\n{}", buffer.text());
    eprintln!("[TEST] Cursor on line {}", buffer.cursor().row);
    
    buffer.duplicate_line();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] After duplicate_line:\n{}", buffer.text());
    
    assert_eq!(buffer.len_lines(), 4, "Should have 4 lines now");
    
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    eprintln!("[TEST] Lines: {:?}", lines);
    
    assert_eq!(lines[1], "line 2");
    assert_eq!(lines[2], "line 2", "Duplicated line should match");
    
    // Test undo
    buffer.undo();
    assert_eq!(buffer.len_lines(), 3);
    eprintln!("[TEST] After undo: {} lines", buffer.len_lines());
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: duplicate_line works with undo");
}

#[test]
fn test_duplicate_last_line_no_newline() {
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("line 1\nline 2");  // No trailing newline
    buffer.goto_line(1);  // Last line
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Text (no trailing newline): {:?}", buffer.text());
    
    buffer.duplicate_line();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] After duplicate: {:?}", buffer.text());
    
    assert_eq!(buffer.len_lines(), 3);
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    assert_eq!(lines[1], "line 2");
    assert_eq!(lines[2], "line 2");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: duplicate_line handles last line without newline");
}

#[test]
fn test_move_line_up() {
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("line 1\nline 2\nline 3");
    buffer.goto_line(1);  // "line 2"
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Initial text:\n{}", buffer.text());
    eprintln!("[TEST] Moving line 2 up");
    
    buffer.move_line_up();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] After move_line_up:\n{}", buffer.text());
    eprintln!("[TEST] Cursor now on line {}", buffer.cursor().row);
    
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    assert_eq!(lines[0], "line 2", "Line 2 should be first now");
    assert_eq!(lines[1], "line 1", "Line 1 should be second now");
    assert_eq!(buffer.cursor().row, 0, "Cursor should follow line up");
    
    // Test undo
    buffer.undo();
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    assert_eq!(lines[0], "line 1");
    eprintln!("[TEST] After undo: first line is {:?}", lines[0]);
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: move_line_up works with undo");
}

#[test]
fn test_move_line_down() {
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("line 1\nline 2\nline 3");
    buffer.goto_line(1);  // "line 2"
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Initial text:\n{}", buffer.text());
    eprintln!("[TEST] Moving line 2 down");
    
    buffer.move_line_down();
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] After move_line_down:\n{}", buffer.text());
    
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    assert_eq!(lines[1], "line 3", "Line 3 should be second now");
    assert_eq!(lines[2], "line 2", "Line 2 should be third now");
    assert_eq!(buffer.cursor().row, 2, "Cursor should follow line down");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: move_line_down works");
}

#[test]
fn test_move_line_at_boundaries() {
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("first\nmiddle\nlast");
    
    // Move first line up - should be no-op
    buffer.goto_line(0);
    buffer.move_line_up();
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    assert_eq!(lines[0], "first", "First line can't move up");
    eprintln!("[TEST] move_line_up on first line is no-op");
    
    // Move last line down - should be no-op
    buffer.goto_line(2);
    buffer.move_line_down();
    let lines: Vec<&str> = buffer.text().lines().collect();
    assert_eq!(lines[2], "last", "Last line can't move down");
    eprintln!("[TEST] move_line_down on last line is no-op");
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: Line move operations handle boundaries");
}

#[test]
fn test_line_operations_preserve_cursor_column() {
    let mut buffer = EditBuffer::with_text("short\nthis is a longer line\nend");
    buffer.goto_line(1);
    buffer.move_to(1, 10);  // Middle of "longer line"
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] Cursor at row={} col={}", buffer.cursor().row, buffer.cursor().col);
    
    buffer.duplicate_line();
    
    // Cursor should stay on original line
    eprintln!("[TEST] After duplicate: row={} col={}", buffer.cursor().row, buffer.cursor().col);
    
    eprintln!("[TEST] PASS: Line operations preserve cursor column");
}

 {"id":"bd-j7g","title":"Terminal Capability Queries","description":"## Overview
Send actual capability queries to the terminal instead of just checking environment variables. This enables accurate feature detection for advanced terminal features.

## Scope
Query sequences to send (per spec Section 2.7):
- DA1 (Primary Device Attributes): `ESC[c`
- DA2 (Secondary Device Attributes): `ESC[>c`
- XTVERSION: `ESC[>0q`
- Pixel resolution: `ESC[14t`
- Kitty keyboard protocol: `ESC[?u`

Parse responses to detect:
- Terminal type and version
- True color support
- Kitty keyboard protocol support
- Synchronized output support
- Sixel graphics support
- SGR pixel mouse mode

## Implementation Details

### Query Sequences
```rust
// In src/terminal/queries.rs
pub const DA1_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[c";           // Primary device attributes
pub const DA2_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[>c";          // Secondary device attributes
pub const XTVERSION_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[>0q";   // Terminal version
pub const PIXEL_SIZE_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[14t";  // Window pixel size

```

### Response Parser
```rust
impl Terminal {
    /// Send capability queries and collect responses.
    /// Uses timeout to avoid blocking if terminal doesn't respond.
    pub fn query_capabilities(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> io::Result<()>;

    /// Parse a terminal response sequence.
    fn parse_response(&mut self, response: &[u8]) -> Option<TerminalResponse>;

    /// Check for pending responses (non-blocking).
    pub fn poll_responses(&mut self) -> Vec<TerminalResponse>;
}

pub enum TerminalResponse {
    DeviceAttributes { 
        primary: bool,  // DA1 vs DA2
        params: Vec<u32>,
    },
    XtVersion { 
        name: String, 
        version: String,
    },
    PixelSize { 
        width: u16, 
        height: u16,
    },
    KittyKeyboard { 
        flags: u32,
    },
    Unknown(Vec<u8>),
}
```

### Response Format Parsing
```
DA1 response: ESC [ ? Ps ; Ps ; ... c
  - Parse params to detect features (e.g., 4 = sixel)
  
DA2 response: ESC [ > Pp ; Pv ; Pc c
  - Pp = terminal type, Pv = firmware version, Pc = ROM cartridge
  
XTVERSION response: ESC P > | text ST
  - text contains terminal name and version
  
Pixel size response: ESC [ 4 ; height ; width t
  - Window size in pixels

Kitty keyboard response: ESC [ ? flags u
  - flags indicates supported modes
```

### Capability Updates
Map responses to Capabilities struct updates:
- DA1 param 4 → sixel = true
- XTVERSION contains "kitty" → kitty_keyboard = true, kitty_graphics = true
- XTVERSION contains "foot"/"alacritty"/"wezterm" → rgb = true
- Pixel size response → store for sixel/image calculations

- src/terminal/mod.rs - add query methods and response handling (~150 lines)
- src/terminal/capabilities.rs - update from parsed responses (~50 lines)

 {"id":"bd-jfno","title":"demo_showcase tests: core logic unit suite (layout/config/palette/log models)","description":"# demo_showcase Tests — Core Logic “Unit” Suite (Headless Checks)

## Purpose

We want unit-level confidence for the demo’s core logic without requiring a real TTY.

Because most demo code lives in the binary, the most reliable approach is to expose deterministic headless “checks” and validate them from integration tests.

This bead adds tests that cover:

- CLI/config parsing (flag combinations)
- layout math invariants (no negative rects, no overflow, compact mode triggers)
- command palette scoring + selection behavior
- log model behavior (ring buffer, selection, hyperlink row formatting)
- hit ID mapping invariants (IDs stable, no overlap across ranges)

## Approach

Extend headless support with a deterministic sub-mode that runs specific checks and emits JSON:

- --headless-check <name>

Examples:

- --headless-check layout
- --headless-check palette
- --headless-check hitgrid

Each check should:

- run without a TTY
- produce structured JSON with:
  - inputs
  - outputs
  - key derived values
- exit 0 on pass, non-zero on failure

Integration tests then:

- run cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --headless-check <name> --headless-dump-json
- snapshot the JSON with insta

## Logging

- Always print a short summary line to stdout.
- On failure, dump detailed context to stderr.
- When HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1, write JSON to target/test-artifacts.

 {"id":"bd-jqv","title":"demo_showcase UI: tour overlay + spotlight (step HUD + highlight)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Tour Overlay + Spotlight (Step HUD + Highlight)

## Purpose

Tour mode is a core “unattended demo” deliverable. Visually, it needs a HUD that makes the tour understandable:

- what step we’re on
- what feature is being demonstrated
- what the viewer should notice

And it needs a “spotlight” effect so the viewer’s eye goes to the right area.

## Visual Requirements

- A compact step HUD overlay (top-right or centered) with:
  - step title
  - step counter (e.g., `3/12`)
  - short callout text (“Notice the alpha blended glass overlay”) 
  - controls: `Next`, `Pause`, `Exit` (clickable)

- A spotlight/highlight effect:
  - dim the entire screen with a translucent tint
  - “punch out” or brighten the target rectangle (panel or UI element)
  - optionally add an animated outline/glow around the target

Implementation note: since we don’t have true compositing masks, approximate punch-out by drawing dim backdrop first, then redraw the target region at full opacity and add a border.

## Input

- Keyboard: `Space` next, `P` pause, `Esc` exit tour
- Mouse: clicking `Next/Pause/Exit` uses HitGrid

 {"id":"bd-po1","title":"demo_showcase runtime: render pass orchestration (z-order, scissor/opacity conventions)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Render Pass Orchestration

## Purpose

Define a consistent render pipeline so the demo has a stable “z-order” and the code remains understandable.

## Proposed Pass Order (Back-to-Front)

1. **Background**
   - Fill screen with `BG0`
   - Subtle gradient noise/pulse (low alpha)

2. **Chrome**
   - Top bar (gradient header)
   - Status bar (key hints + stats)

3. **Panels** (each clipped)
   - Sidebar
   - Editor
   - Preview
   - Logs

4. **Overlays** (semi-transparent, “glass”)
   - Help
   - Command palette
   - Tour overlay

5. **Toasts**
   - Small ephemeral notifications stacked in a corner

6. **Debug / Perf Overlay**
   - OpenTUI’s `Renderer::set_debug_overlay(true)` is separate, but we can also draw our own lightweight stats.

## Scissor + Opacity Conventions

- Every panel uses:
  - `push_scissor(inner_rect)` before content
  - optional `push_opacity(alpha)` for “glass” content areas
  - then pop in reverse order

- Overlays:
  - full-screen scissor
  - first draw dimming rectangle with opacity
  - then draw modal panel

## Grapheme Pool Conventions

- Default UI text can use `buffer.draw_text`.
- Any place we want to **prove multi-codepoint graphemes** must use pool-aware drawing:
  - `let (buf, pool) = renderer.buffer_with_pool();`
  - `buf.draw_text_with_pool(pool, ...)` or `TextBufferView::render_to_with_pool(...)`

## HitGrid Conventions

- `renderer.clear()` already clears the hit grid.
- While rendering, register hit areas *after* layout is computed.
- For nested hit areas, use `push_hit_scissor()` matching the visual scissor so clicks align with clipped visuals.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:
